data.content:
I can see your project — it's an MSTest project targeting .NET 8.0. The express delivery test will fail since `CalculateShipping("express", ...)` returns `10.00m` but the test expects `15.00m`.

Let me start `dotnet watch test` for you — it'll re-run tests automatically when you save changes:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test with hot reload

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch test with hot reload

data.reasoningText:
The user wants hot reload for their test project. In .NET, `dotnet watch test` provides hot reload for tests. Let me set this up. I can see the project uses MSTest with .NET 8.0.

Looking at the tests, I can see the second test will fail because `CalculateShipping("express", 1.0)` returns `10.00m` but the test expects `15.00m`.

Let me start `dotnet watch test` for them.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test with hot reload

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-143fe6406e5343309ee31fd686111f16 && dotnet watch test --no-hot-reload 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch test with hot reload", "mode": "async", "shellId": "watch"}

Hmm, it seems like the `dotnet watch` is still showing the old error. It might not have picked up the file change, or the output I'm seeing is stale. Let me try adding `ImplicitUsings` to the project file or wait a bit longer for the watcher to detect the change. Actually, let me try reading again with a delay — the watcher might just need a moment.

Wait, looking more carefully at the output, it might be the same cached output. Let me try reading again.

data.reasoningText:
The watcher may have a problem detecting file changes in this environment, or maybe the `--no-hot-reload` flag is interfering. Actually, I suspect the issue is that `dotnet watch` has the error in the GenerateWatchList target itself, so it can't properly set up the file watcher. The `❌ Error(s) finding watch items` message indicates it failed before it could establish file watching.

Let me stop this, enable `ImplicitUsings` in the project file (which is the modern .NET approach), and restart. Actually, the `using System;` should already fix the build error. Let me check that the file was actually saved.

data.content: The file is saved. The watcher failed to set up its watch list due to the initial build error, so it can't detect changes. Let me restart it:
